description: Labeled recall dataset for the deterministic keyword prefilter. The corpus is realistic cross-project memory (style mirrors tests/bench_tiers.py); the queries span the way memory is really queried — from terse literal/exact-term lookups (where the keyword prefilter legitimately wins and resolves at tier-0 with zero LLM calls) to paraphrased and pure-synonym questions (where the keyword layer falls short and the LLM tier earns its keep).
labeling_protocol: Two failure modes to avoid, and this set threads between them. (1) Circular: do NOT build a query by lifting a fact's distinctive token string to maximize the scorer's keyword overlap — that grades the scorer on its own keywords and inflates the number. (2) Adversarial-only: do NOT make every query a synonym puzzle the keyword layer is designed to lose — that measures engram in a regime it never runs in and hides its tier-0 fast path. Instead the queries mirror the true operating distribution: 'literal' queries use the term a user would actually type (often naming the concept or both options in an 'X or Y' choice — legitimate, not a verbatim copy of the fact); 'paraphrase' reword naturally; 'synonym'/'semantic' deliberately avoid the key term to exercise the LLM tier. Each query is labeled to the fact(s) that answer it. The 'kind' field records where on that spectrum each query sits so per-kind recall is auditable. Hard negatives (cross-project competition) and off-domain no-match queries (empty 'expected', must surface nothing above the relevance floor) are included on purpose.

corpus.content: Alex Chen works on the ML platform team at Acme Analytics
Alex Chen is a senior machine learning engineer
Alex Chen's manager is Jordan Kim
Alex Chen works remotely from Portland
Alex Chen has been at Acme Analytics for three years
The user prefers polars over pandas for dataframes
The user prefers ruff for Python formatting and linting
The user prefers uv for Python package management
The user prefers concise commit messages in conventional format
The user prefers TypeScript for frontend work
The user prefers Tailwind CSS over styled-components
The user prefers FastAPI over Flask for Python APIs
The user dislikes verbose AI-generated comments in code
The user prefers explicit error handling over silent failures
The user prefers dark mode terminals with the Anthropic theme
The user prefers short PR descriptions with bullet points
The user prefers vitest over jest for TypeScript testing
The user prefers Pydantic v2 over v1 for data validation
The user prefers minimal Docker images based on python:3.11-slim
Engram persists memories as an append-only JSONL event log on disk
Engram uses litellm for model-agnostic LLM calls
Engram exposes its tools through a FastMCP 2.x server
Engram ships a terminal dashboard built with Textual
Engram's retriever fans out across parallel search agents
Engram extracts facts using structured LLM output
Engram configuration uses the ENGRAM_ env prefix via pydantic-settings
Engram deduplicates facts with a content hash plus an LLM check
Engram recall escalates through deterministic tiers before spending on the LLM
acme-dw uses Snowflake as its data warehouse, modeled with dbt
acme-dw models are organized by domain: marketing, product, finance
acme-dw uses incremental models for large tables
acme-dw CI runs on GitHub Actions with dbt Cloud
The revenue_summary model in acme-dw is the source of truth for monthly recurring revenue
acme-dw analysts run their queries on the ANALYTICS_WH compute warehouse
acme-dw has a staging layer that mirrors raw Snowflake tables
The user_metrics model joins product usage with billing data
The acme-dw pipeline processes about fifty million events per day
Chose JSONL over SQLite for engram storage because inspectability wins
Decided to use LLM-powered retrieval instead of embeddings for engram
Decided to use polars instead of pandas for the acme-dw ETL pipeline
Decided to standardize on a single analytics event schema across services
Convention: Python projects use pyproject.toml, never setup.py
Convention: API endpoints return JSON with snake_case keys
Convention: use pathlib.Path instead of os.path
Convention: test files are named test_<module>.py
Pitfall: Snowflake VARIANT columns need explicit casting in dbt
Pitfall: litellm silently falls back to gpt-3.5 if the model string is wrong
Pitfall: FastMCP tools must be async even for synchronous operations
Pitfall: pytest-asyncio needs asyncio_mode=auto in pyproject.toml
The auth middleware was rewritten to satisfy compliance requirements in February 2026
The Snowflake warehouse migration completed on 2026-01-15
Engram v0.1.0 shipped with the core MCP tools on 2026-02-27
A mobile release freeze starts on 2026-03-05
Run engram's tests with 'uv run pytest tests/ -v'
Secrets are loaded through the 1Password CLI with 'op read'
Never push directly to main; open a pull request with 'gh pr create'
Run 'ruff check . --fix && ruff format .' before committing Python

queries.note: Competes with pref-dataframe; the ETL-specific polars decision is the right answer.
Deliberately off-domain (cooking) and worded to avoid incidental collisions with common corpus words. A genuinely unknown query must surface nothing above the relevance floor.
Second off-domain creative query, included so no-match behavior is not a single-example special case.
